title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered with urban cycling initiatives. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Emily, The Route Tester
Emily volunteered with a cycling group that checked new local routes before recommending them to beginners on the group's printed map. [47]She refused to approve one attractive riverside path because riders had to cross two lanes of fast morning traffic at the end.[/47] Some members thought she was being negative, but a later near-miss proved her caution useful. Emily had worked as a driving instructor, and [48]that background helped her predict where nervous cyclists would look at the wrong moment.[/48] She carried a small waterproof notebook and rated each route for lighting, surface quality, parked cars and places to stop safely without blocking pedestrians. What she enjoyed most was seeing adults who had not cycled since childhood complete their first quiet local loop. Emily says a good route is not the shortest one; it is the one that leaves people willing to ride again tomorrow, even without a group leader.
texts.B:
### Marco, The Maintenance Coach
Marco ran free basic bicycle checks outside the library on Saturday mornings, using a folding stand borrowed from a shop. [49]He soon realised that many people called any strange noise a brake problem, even when the chain was dry or the mudguard was loose.[/49] Instead of fixing everything himself, he asked owners to hold the tool and feel what changed. Marco had been a mechanic for delivery bikes, so he knew how quickly a small fault could become expensive or unsafe if ignored. [50]His proudest repair was adjusting a child's donated bike so perfectly that its new owner rode circles round the square for half an hour.[/50] He disliked the pressure of long queues, especially when rain was forecast and people arrived all at once. Still, he believed the regular checks mattered because a reliable bicycle made commuting seem possible for people who could not afford bus fares every day.
texts.C:
### Anita, The Confidence Leader
Anita patiently led short gentle practice rides for adults who were afraid of cycling in traffic after years away from bikes. [51]She began every session in an empty car park because several participants had never signalled while turning their heads.[/51] Her rule was that nobody had to apologise for being slow, wobbly or confused. As a yoga teacher, she used breathing exercises before busy unfamiliar junctions, which some riders first found ridiculous and later requested before every difficult turn. [52]The hardest participant to support was a man who laughed at his own fear so loudly that others could not admit theirs.[/52] Anita spoke to him privately, and the atmosphere improved almost immediately. She says progress was often invisible: a rider placing both feet calmly on the pedals, or choosing a quieter side street without needing permission. She now keeps rides small enough for everyone to be noticed and encouraged individually.
texts.D:
### Noor, The School Streets Organiser
Noor successfully campaigned for temporary traffic restrictions twice each week outside a crowded primary school at morning drop-off time, when pavements were crowded. [53]She first became involved after her nephew was nearly hit by a reversing van near the gate.[/53] The project required exceptional patience because some parents saw the restriction as criticism of their normal routines. Noor created a weekly rota of volunteers wearing bright yellow vests, and [54]she deliberately placed the friendliest people at the barriers to prevent arguments from escalating.[/54] Her marketing job helped her design posters that explained the change with humour rather than blame or technical language. The most convincing evidence came when several teachers reported that children arrived calmer on car-free mornings, even on wet days. [55]Noor found the permit forms harder than the public meetings.[/55] [56]She wants the scheme to continue without volunteers eventually, as a normal part of the street's permanent design.[/56]

questions.question: rejected a route because of a dangerous crossing at its conclusion?
questions.explanation: Emily would not approve a riverside path because it ended with a fast traffic crossing.

questions.question: used previous road-safety experience to anticipate beginners' mistakes?
questions.explanation: Emily's driving instructor background helped her predict where nervous cyclists would look wrongly.

questions.question: noticed that people misidentified the source of mechanical sounds?
questions.explanation: Marco found people often described any strange noise as a brake problem.

questions.question: was especially proud of making a donated bike fit its young rider well?
questions.explanation: Marco adjusted a child's donated bike so its new owner could ride around the square.

questions.question: started sessions away from traffic to practise a specific signalling skill?
questions.explanation: Anita began in a car park because participants had not signalled while turning their heads.

questions.question: had to deal with someone whose joking prevented others from admitting anxiety?
questions.explanation: Anita supported a man whose loud laughter about his fear stopped others admitting theirs.

questions.question: joined after a young relative was almost injured near a school?
questions.explanation: Noor became involved after her nephew was nearly hit near the school gate.

questions.question: chose particular volunteers for confrontational positions because of their manner?
questions.explanation: Noor put the friendliest people at barriers to stop arguments escalating.

questions.question: found official paperwork more difficult than public discussion?
questions.explanation: Noor found permit forms harder than public meetings.

questions.question: hopes the change will eventually work without volunteer staffing?
questions.explanation: Noor wants the scheme to continue as street design rather than relying on volunteers.
